title: NIST CSF v2 — GOVERN (GV)
content:
GOVERN (GV) is the function added in NIST Cybersecurity Framework v2 (published 2024-02-26). It establishes the cybersecurity risk management strategy, expectations, and policy that frame how all other functions operate. GOVERN is positioned at the centre of the CSF v2 wheel because every other function (IDENTIFY, PROTECT, DETECT, RESPOND, RECOVER) inherits direction from it.

GOVERN covers six categories: GV.OC Organizational Context, GV.RM Risk Management Strategy, GV.RR Roles Responsibilities and Authorities, GV.PO Policy, GV.OV Oversight, and GV.SC Cybersecurity Supply Chain Risk Management.

Key sub-categories: GV.OC-01 (the organization's mission is understood and informs cybersecurity risk management), GV.OC-02 (internal and external stakeholders are understood, and their needs and expectations regarding cybersecurity risk management are understood and considered), GV.RM-01 (risk management objectives are established and agreed to by organizational stakeholders), GV.RM-03 (cybersecurity risk management activities and outcomes are included in enterprise risk management processes), GV.RR-01 (organizational leadership is responsible and accountable for cybersecurity risk), GV.RR-02 (roles, responsibilities, and authorities related to cybersecurity risk management are established, communicated, understood, and enforced), GV.PO-01 (policy for managing cybersecurity risks is established based on organizational context, cybersecurity strategy, and priorities and is communicated and enforced), GV.OV-01 (cybersecurity risk management strategy outcomes are reviewed to inform and adjust strategy and direction), GV.SC-01 (a cybersecurity supply chain risk management program, strategy, objectives, policies, and processes are established and agreed to by organizational stakeholders), GV.SC-06 (planning and due diligence are performed to reduce risks before entering into formal supplier or other third-party relationships), GV.SC-10 (cybersecurity supply chain risk management plans include provisions for activities that occur after the conclusion of a partnership or service agreement).

GV.SC is the most consequential addition for CISOs: NIST formalised supply-chain risk as a first-class governance concern, aligning with DORA Article 28 (ICT third-party risk) and NIS2 supply-chain security obligations. Boards now expect CISO reporting against GV.OV outcomes; CISOs use GV.RR to anchor reporting lines to the CEO or board audit committee.

Members of the management body must actively keep up-to-date with sufficient knowledge and skills to understand and assess ICT risk and its impact. ESAs (the European Supervisory Authorities — EBA, EIOPA, ESMA) have signalled that this implies formal cyber-literacy training for board members at scope entities.

Proportionality: smaller and less complex entities benefit from simplified ICT risk management framework requirements per Art. 16, but Art. 5 governance obligations apply in full regardless of size.

Scope: leadership accountability, policy, risk strategy, third-party risk governance. Use this function when discussing board reporting, vendor risk programs, or how cybersecurity ties into enterprise risk management.

title: NIST CSF v2 — IDENTIFY (ID)
content:
IDENTIFY (ID) develops an organizational understanding to manage cybersecurity risk to systems, people, assets, data, and capabilities. In CSF v2, IDENTIFY was streamlined to three categories: ID.AM Asset Management, ID.RA Risk Assessment, and ID.IM Improvement (the latter newly elevated to a top-level category).

Key sub-categories: ID.AM-01 (inventories of hardware managed by the organization are maintained), ID.AM-02 (inventories of software, services, and systems managed by the organization are maintained), ID.AM-03 (representations of the organization's authorized network communication and internal and external network data flows are maintained), ID.AM-04 (inventories of services provided by suppliers are maintained), ID.AM-05 (assets are prioritized based on classification, criticality, resources, and impact on the mission), ID.AM-07 (inventories of data and corresponding metadata for designated data types are maintained), ID.AM-08 (systems, hardware, software, services, and data are managed throughout their life cycles), ID.RA-01 (vulnerabilities in assets are identified, validated, and recorded), ID.RA-02 (cyber threat intelligence is received from information sharing forums and sources), ID.RA-03 (internal and external threats to the organization are identified and recorded), ID.RA-04 (potential impacts and likelihoods of threats exploiting vulnerabilities are identified and recorded), ID.RA-05 (threats, vulnerabilities, likelihoods, and impacts are used to understand inherent risk and inform risk response prioritization), ID.RA-09 (the authenticity and integrity of hardware and software are assessed prior to acquisition and use), ID.IM-01 (improvements are identified from evaluations), ID.IM-04 (incident response plans and other cybersecurity plans that affect operations are established, communicated, maintained, and improved).

Asset management (ID.AM) is the most frequently failing area in real-world assessments: CISOs report sub-60% confidence in their software and data inventories. ID.AM-04 (supplier service inventories) and ID.AM-08 (asset lifecycle management) were strengthened in v2 to acknowledge that modern attack surfaces include SaaS, APIs, and shadow IT that traditional CMDB tools miss.

ID.IM Improvement is new in v2 as a top-level category — NIST elevated continuous improvement so it's not buried inside other functions. ID.IM-01 specifically calls for improvements from tabletop exercises, post-incident reviews, and audit findings to feed back into the program.

Scope: asset inventories, threat and vulnerability identification, risk assessment, continuous improvement. Use this function when discussing CMDB completeness, vulnerability management programs, third-party inventories, or supply-chain integrity validation.

title: NIST CSF v2 — PROTECT (PR)
content:
PROTECT (PR) implements the safeguards that enable the organization to limit or contain the impact of a potential cybersecurity event. CSF v2 reorganized PROTECT into five categories: PR.AA Identity Management Authentication and Access Control, PR.AT Awareness and Training, PR.DS Data Security, PR.PS Platform Security, and PR.IR Technology Infrastructure Resilience.

Key sub-categories: PR.AA-01 (identities and credentials for authorized users, services, and hardware are managed by the organization), PR.AA-02 (identities are proofed and bound to credentials based on the context of interactions), PR.AA-03 (users, services, and hardware are authenticated), PR.AA-05 (access permissions, entitlements, and authorizations are defined in a policy, managed, enforced, and reviewed, and incorporate the principles of least privilege and separation of duties), PR.AA-06 (physical access to assets is managed, monitored, and enforced commensurate with risk), PR.AT-01 (personnel are provided with awareness and training so that they possess the knowledge and skills to perform general tasks with cybersecurity risks in mind), PR.AT-02 (individuals in specialized roles are provided with awareness and training so that they possess the knowledge and skills to perform relevant tasks with cybersecurity risks in mind), PR.DS-01 (the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data-at-rest are protected), PR.DS-02 (the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data-in-transit are protected), PR.DS-10 (the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data-in-use are protected), PR.DS-11 (backups of data are created, protected, maintained, and tested), PR.PS-01 (configuration management practices are established and applied), PR.PS-02 (software is maintained, replaced, and removed commensurate with risk), PR.PS-03 (hardware is maintained, replaced, and removed commensurate with risk), PR.PS-05 (installation and execution of unauthorized software are prevented), PR.PS-06 (secure software development practices are integrated, and their performance is monitored throughout the software development life cycle), PR.IR-01 (networks and environments are protected from unauthorized logical access and usage), PR.IR-03 (mechanisms are implemented to achieve resilience requirements in normal and adverse situations), PR.IR-04 (adequate resource capacity to ensure availability is maintained).

PR.PT (Protective Technology) from CSF v1 was largely merged into PR.PS Platform Security in v2. PR.IR is new as an explicit resilience-of-infrastructure category, aligned with DORA's operational resilience requirements.

PR.AA is where identity becomes load-bearing — PR.AA-05 (least privilege and separation of duties) is the basis for zero-trust architecture discussions. PR.DS-11 (backup testing) is the control most frequently failed during ransomware tabletop exercises.

Scope: identity and access management, data protection, secure configuration, software maintenance, infrastructure resilience. Use this function when discussing zero trust, encryption, patch management, backup strategy, or developer security practices.

title: NIST CSF v2 — DETECT (DE)
content:
DETECT (DE) develops and implements activities to identify the occurrence of a cybersecurity event in a timely manner. CSF v2 consolidated DETECT into two categories: DE.CM Continuous Monitoring and DE.AE Adverse Event Analysis.

Key sub-categories: DE.CM-01 (networks and network services are monitored to find potentially adverse events), DE.CM-02 (the physical environment is monitored to find potentially adverse events), DE.CM-03 (personnel activity and technology usage are monitored to find potentially adverse events), DE.CM-06 (external service provider activities and services are monitored to find potentially adverse events), DE.CM-09 (computing hardware and software, runtime environments, and their data are monitored to find potentially adverse events), DE.AE-02 (potentially adverse events are analyzed to better understand associated activities), DE.AE-03 (information is correlated from multiple sources), DE.AE-04 (the estimated impact and scope of adverse events are understood), DE.AE-06 (information on adverse events is provided to authorized staff and tools), DE.AE-07 (cyber threat intelligence and other contextual information are integrated into the analysis), DE.AE-08 (incidents are declared when adverse events meet the defined incident criteria).

DE.CM-06 (monitoring external service provider activities) was strengthened in v2 to address the reality that ~30% of breaches now come through trusted third parties. DE.AE-07 (integrating cyber threat intelligence into event analysis) formalises what mature SOCs already do but creates pressure on smaller organisations to subscribe to actionable threat feeds.

The DETECT function is where SIEM, EDR/XDR, and CSPM tools live. CSF v2 deliberately avoided naming technology categories, but in practice DE.CM-01/09 maps to network monitoring + EDR/CWPP, DE.AE-03 maps to SIEM correlation, and DE.AE-07 maps to threat intelligence integration.

DE.AE-08 (incident declaration criteria) is the most frequently missing control — many organisations have SIEM correlation but lack a documented threshold at which an event becomes an incident. This gap creates legal exposure under DORA (32-hour reporting clock) and NIS2 (24-hour early warning).

Scope: continuous monitoring, event correlation, incident detection, threat intelligence integration. Use this function when discussing SIEM/EDR/XDR investments, SOC maturity, MDR services, or how detection feeds into the incident response process.

title: NIST CSF v2 — RESPOND (RS)
content:
RESPOND (RS) takes action regarding a detected cybersecurity incident to contain its impact. CSF v2 reorganized RESPOND into four categories: RS.MA Incident Management, RS.AN Incident Analysis, RS.CO Incident Response Reporting and Communication, and RS.MI Incident Mitigation.

Key sub-categories: RS.MA-01 (the incident response plan is executed in coordination with relevant third parties once an incident is declared), RS.MA-02 (incident reports are triaged and validated), RS.MA-03 (incidents are categorized and prioritized), RS.MA-04 (incidents are escalated or elevated as needed), RS.MA-05 (the criteria for initiating incident recovery are applied), RS.AN-03 (analysis is performed to establish what has taken place during an incident and the root cause of the incident), RS.AN-06 (actions performed during an investigation are recorded and the records' integrity and provenance are preserved), RS.AN-07 (incident data and metadata are collected, and their integrity and provenance are preserved), RS.AN-08 (an incident's magnitude is estimated and validated), RS.CO-02 (internal and external stakeholders are notified of incidents), RS.CO-03 (information is shared with designated internal and external stakeholders), RS.MI-01 (incidents are contained), RS.MI-02 (incidents are eradicated).

RS.CO-02 (stakeholder notification) is now a legal pressure point in multiple regimes: SEC's 4-day disclosure for material incidents (Item 1.05 of Form 8-K, effective 2023-12-15), DORA's 24-hour initial classification + 72-hour intermediate report + final report by 1 month, NIS2's 24-hour early warning + 72-hour incident notification + 1-month final report, and HIPAA's 60-day breach notification rule. CISOs use RS.CO-02 to anchor the regulatory reporting decision tree.

RS.AN-06 and RS.AN-07 (forensic record integrity) are where chain-of-custody discipline lives — increasingly important as incident data is used in regulatory filings and litigation. Tools that automatically preserve provenance (EDR forensic snapshots, immutable SIEM storage) are scored against these controls.

RS.MI-01 (containment) and RS.MI-02 (eradication) are the technical work most CISO panels focus on, but the regulatory and communications work (RS.CO) increasingly dominates the time of CISOs at incident-impacted organisations.

Scope: incident response execution, forensic analysis, regulatory and stakeholder communication, containment and eradication. Use this function when discussing IR playbooks, breach notification obligations, retainer services (DFIR), or incident severity classification.

title: NIST CSF v2 — RECOVER (RC)
content:
RECOVER (RC) maintains plans for resilience and restores any capabilities or services that were impaired due to a cybersecurity incident. CSF v2 organized RECOVER into two categories: RC.RP Incident Recovery Plan Execution and RC.CO Incident Recovery Communication.

Key sub-categories: RC.RP-01 (the recovery portion of the incident response plan is executed once initiated from the incident response process), RC.RP-02 (recovery actions are selected, scoped, prioritized, and performed), RC.RP-03 (the integrity of backups and other restoration assets is verified before using them for restoration), RC.RP-04 (critical mission functions and cybersecurity risk management are considered to establish post-incident operational norms), RC.RP-05 (the integrity of restored assets is verified, systems and services are restored, and normal operating status is confirmed), RC.RP-06 (the end of incident recovery is declared based on criteria, and incident-related documentation is completed), RC.CO-03 (recovery activities and progress in restoring operational capabilities are communicated to designated internal and external stakeholders), RC.CO-04 (public updates on incident recovery are shared using approved methods and messaging).

RC.RP-03 (backup integrity verification before restore) is the control that most often saves organisations from ransomware paying — and most often fails the first time it's tested. NIST's CSF v2 implementation guide explicitly warns against assuming backups are clean: the verification step must include both integrity hashes and behavioral validation (does the restored system actually function correctly, and is the attacker's persistence absent).

RC.RP-04 (post-incident operational norms) acknowledges that 'returning to normal' often means a new normal — new compensating controls, new monitoring, new vendor relationships. Mature recovery programs document this explicitly so the organization doesn't drift back into the pre-incident risk posture.

RC.CO-04 (public updates) ties to SEC disclosure obligations and customer/regulator communications during prolonged outages. Companies that have failed publicly during recovery (CrowdStrike's 2024-07 global outage, Change Healthcare's 2024 ransomware) cite RC.CO-04 as the area where their communications discipline broke down.

Recovery time objective (RTO) and recovery point objective (RPO) targets sit under RC.RP — these are tested via DR exercises and validated against business impact analysis from ID.RA. DORA Article 12 (ICT business continuity policy) and ICT response and recovery plans map directly to RC.RP requirements.

Scope: recovery plan execution, backup integrity validation, post-incident new-normal documentation, recovery communications. Use this function when discussing disaster recovery exercises, ransomware preparedness, RTO/RPO targets, or post-incident reporting to boards and regulators.
